summary: Gruber examines Amazon affiliate data showing that 171 out of 579 Daring Fireball readers pre-ordering Mac OS X Leopard chose the five-license family pack over the single-license version. He notes that since Apple doesn't enforce the single-license restriction in code, many buyers are paying more simply out of honesty. He contrasts Apple's trust-based approach with Microsoft's intrusive Windows activation system, expressing gratitude that Apple doesn't tie licenses to hardware identifiers. The post suggests that if DF readers are at all representative, family packs are a significant revenue success for Apple built on customer goodwill.
key_points: Of 579 Leopard pre-orders through DF affiliate links, nearly 30% chose the more expensive family pack despite no technical enforcement of the single-license restriction
Apple's decision not to enforce single-license restrictions in code creates a trust relationship with users that appears to pay off commercially
Microsoft's Windows activation system is presented as the cautionary alternative — intrusive DRM that annoys honest customers
Apple's hardware lock-in (macOS only runs on Macs) reduces piracy risk enough that aggressive license enforcement is unnecessary
money_quotes: What's interesting about this is that the single-computer license isn't enforced in code by the operating system.
Which means many readers are doing the right thing simply because they're honest.
I have no idea if this breakdown is representative of the Mac user base as a whole, but if it's even close, these family packs are a huge success for Apple.
Every time I read stories about the annoyance of dealing with Microsoft's Windows activation, I'm thankful that Apple doesn't do anything like that with Mac OS X.
But they could enforce these licenses with serial-checking code, and they could tie serial numbers to machine-specific hardware identifiers. And I'm thankful they don't.
key_insight: Apple's decision to trust its customers rather than enforce licenses through intrusive activation schemes is rewarded by honest purchasing behavior and customer goodwill.
